learn.title: Duck Curve: Solar's Sunset Challenge
learn.intro: The duck curve describes how solar power generation drops sharply at sunset, creating a dip in grid supply. This challenges grid stability as demand peaks in the evening. Solving this is critical for integrating renewables and avoiding blackouts, making it a key issue for energy transition.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a duck-shaped graph where solar output (the body) plummets at sunset (the beak). During the day, solar floods the grid, but as the sun sets, supply drops while demand rises. This mismatch forces power plants to ramp up quickly, risking grid instability. For example, California’s grid faces this daily.
learn.c2_text: Solar panels generate less power as sunlight fades, but households and industries still use energy. This sudden gap requires backup power, often from fossil fuels. The US DOE notes this effect can increase emissions if not managed. Grid operators must balance this with storage or demand response.
learn.c3_text: Battery storage can store daytime solar for evening use. India’s 18 GW of grid-scale storage (2024) helps mitigate this. Smart grids use AI to shift demand, like running appliances at night. The US DOE highlights these as essential for a renewable-heavy grid.
learn.stat2: California: 70% of peak demand occurs post-sunset
learn.stat3: US: 40% of solar-generated power is curtailed due to grid limits
learn.stat4: Global: 70% of solar projects require storage to avoid duck curve issues

learn.title: Carbon Markets: Clean Energy Incentives
learn.intro: Carbon markets let companies buy/sell permits to emit CO2. By pricing pollution, they incentivize clean energy adoption. This drives investment in renewables, making it a powerful tool for reducing emissions globally.
learn.c1_text: A carbon credit represents 1 ton of CO2 reduced. Companies can trade these credits. For instance, a factory might buy credits instead of cutting emissions. The IEA states this creates financial motivation to adopt solar or wind, which have zero emissions.
learn.c2_text: If emitting CO2 costs money, businesses switch to renewables. India’s carbon market pilot allows companies to offset emissions via solar projects. The IEA reports that carbon pricing could boost renewable investments by $1 trillion annually by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India’s carbon credit market aims to fund 500 MW of renewable projects by 2025. States like Maharashtra offer incentives for solar farms to generate credits. The NITI Aayog plans to integrate this into national climate goals.
learn.stat1: India: $1.5B allocated for carbon credit projects (2023)
learn.stat2: Global: 1.8B tons of CO2 traded annually via carbon markets
learn.stat4: India: 50 MW solar projects approved under carbon credit scheme (2024)

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells: Clean Vehicle Power
learn.intro: Hydrogen fuel cells generate electricity by combining hydrogen and oxygen, emitting only water. This makes them ideal for zero-emission vehicles. They’re key to decarbonizing transport, especially heavy-duty trucks and buses.
learn.c1_text: Fuel cells work like batteries but use hydrogen and oxygen. When hydrogen reacts, it produces electricity, heat, and water. A single fuel cell stack can power a car for 300 miles. The US DOE notes this is 3-4x more efficient than burning hydrogen in engines.
learn.c2_text: Hydrogen has higher energy density than batteries, allowing longer ranges. Buses in California use hydrogen fuel cells, achieving 1,000-mile ranges. The US DOE highlights that hydrogen can refuel in 5 minutes, faster than charging electric vehicles.
learn.c3_text: India aims to produce 5 million metric tons of green hydrogen by 2030. The PM e-DRIVE scheme includes hydrogen-powered buses in cities like Delhi. The NITI Aayog plans to set up 10 hydrogen refueling stations by 2025.
learn.stat1: India: 10 GW planned green hydrogen production by 2030
learn.stat2: California: 40 hydrogen fuel cell buses in operation
learn.stat3: Global: Hydrogen vehicles could save 6M tons CO2/year by 2030
learn.stat4: India: 500 kg hydrogen produced daily via renewable sources (2024)

learn.title: PPAs: Financing Renewable Projects
learn.intro: Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) let buyers commit to purchasing renewable energy at a fixed price. This reduces risk for developers and ensures stable revenue, accelerating solar and wind projects. PPAs are vital for scaling renewables in India and globally.
learn.c1_text: A PPA is a contract where a buyer (like a company) agrees to buy all or part of a renewable project’s output. For example, a factory might sign a 20-year PPA for solar power. IRENA states this lowers upfront costs for developers, making projects more viable.
learn.c2_text: PPAs transfer financial risk from developers to buyers. If solar panels underperform, the buyer still pays. This encourages investment in high-quality projects. In India, corporate PPAs have grown to 10 GW by 2023, per IRENA.
learn.c3_text: Companies like Tata Power and Adani have secured 5 GW of corporate PPAs in India. States like Gujarat offer tax breaks for PPA-backed projects. The NITI Aayog aims to double PPA capacity to 20 GW by 2025.
learn.stat2: Global: PPAs account for 40% of renewable project financing
learn.stat3: Gujarat: 60% of solar projects use PPAs

learn.title: Solar Panels: Light to Electricity
learn.intro: Solar PV panels convert sunlight into electricity using the photoelectric effect. This process, discovered by Einstein, is the backbone of modern solar technology. It’s a clean, scalable solution for powering homes, industries, and grids worldwide.
learn.c1_text: Solar panels contain silicon cells. When sunlight hits them, electrons are released, creating an electric current. This is the photoelectric effect. A typical 1 kW panel generates 1.5 kWh daily in India. The US DOE explains this is enough to power a home for 8 hours.
learn.c2_text: Modern panels convert 15-22% of sunlight to electricity. Prices have dropped 80% since 2010. In India, a 5 kW system costs ₹1.5 lakh, making solar affordable for 70% of households. The US DOE notes this efficiency makes solar competitive with fossil fuels.
learn.c3_text: India has 70 GW of solar capacity (2024), with 25 GW added in 2023. The PM Suryodaya Yojana aims to install 100 GW by 2025. States like Rajasthan lead with 15 GW, using solar to power rural villages.

learn.title: India EV Policy: PM e-DRIVE Scheme
learn.intro: The PM e-DRIVE scheme promotes electric vehicle adoption in India through subsidies, infrastructure, and policies. It aims to reduce oil imports and emissions. State-level policies complement this, creating a national EV ecosystem critical for India’s clean energy future.
learn.c1_text: PM e-DRIVE provides subsidies for EV buyers, charging infrastructure, and R&D. For example, buyers get ₹15,000 for compact EVs. The scheme targets 30% EV sales by 2030. NITI Aayog states this reduces India’s $100B annual oil spend.
learn.c2_text: Subsidies lower upfront costs, while charging stations ease range anxiety. India has 7,000 public EV charging points (2024). The scheme also funds battery swapping, like in Tamil Nadu, where 500 swap stations operate. This makes EVs practical for daily use.
learn.c3_text: Maharashtra offers free charging at government offices, while Karnataka provides 50% subsidies for EVs. The NITI Aayog plans 40,000 charging stations by 2026. India’s EV sales hit 2.5M in 2023, a 150% jump from 2022.
learn.stat4: India: 30% EV subsidy for commercial vehicles
